speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, OK. Be ready for you in a second there. Mic on you. OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: April 7, 1995. Interviewing Mr. Joseph Horn, nee Hershenhorn, Leslie Fass, interviewer, in Glen Rock, New Jersey, USA. English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll just get the slate here-- slate. Slate in here. OK, good. Get the headset on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good luck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you. I'm not nervous at all, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. I don't have to worry about you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't spare me. You don't have to-- no. The only worry I have is about finding the right words to express myself. Oh, thank you, Ben.

speaker: interviewer
text: All righty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We'll be all set.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whenever you're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all yours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Horn, would you give us your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Joseph Horn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a different name in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born Hershenhorn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you change your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Upon arrival in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Radom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Radom, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-D-O-M.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 15, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell me, please, what life was like for you in Radom, Poland, before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was the youngest of four children. My father was, you might say, maybe lower middle income. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All four children went to private school, which was a terrible burden. But my parents insisted that we do that, because education was the way to go for Jews in Poland. Jews had to be-- just being smart was not good enough. They had to be very smart to succeed in life in Poland. And so all four of us went to private school, which put a burden on the family income. And we struggled, like everybody else, but thank God there was always enough to eat in the house. And there was enough to clothe us, even though it was real tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father worked for a German firm. Radom-- the city of Radom was a leather manufacturing center in Poland, and almost everybody in Radom had something to do with the manufacture or sale of leather. And my father came from a family of people who manufactured leather. That factory, which belonged to my grandfather, my father's father, was sold to a German family sometime before I was born. And my father worked for these people who had bought this factory. And so he-- for quite some time, he traveled throughout the countryside, and he bought leather hides for the factory. That was his primary job. When he came back and he was home, he would sell that leather, which was made and manufactured, from an office warehouse in our town. So some part of the year he would travel to buy the skins, and sometimes he would stay for periods of time at home. And that's how life was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a good Jewish housewife. Took care of us children, which was a tremendous job-- four children, household.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe some of the Jewish rituals that you practiced in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- --we had a kosher home. We were religious, and we all believed in God. My mother was more rigid and Orthodox than my father. As a child, I felt, most of the time, that my father wanted us to go through the motions and that, for him, the ritual of religion was important. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He wanted me to accompany him when he went to shul on Shabbos. And I did that, and so did the other children. But once we got to shul, he didn't care whether I stayed with him or went out to play with boys.

speaker: interviewee
text: So religion, to us, in our family, was more of a tradition than it was, you know, religious. And I grew up that way. And I'm still that way, and I brought up my children that way. Can you describe what a Passover Seder would look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We had the traditional Passover Seder. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a-- it was a terrific thing. It was just great. The tradition of the Seder is one of the most beautiful Jewish religious rites.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was at the table?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family was at the table. Sometimes we had guests. But my father and my mother-- and we moved the table over to the sofa. We had a sofa in the room so that some of us would lean back, according to ritual, and all that. And it was a very, very festive occasion. My mother would work hard and prepare for the Passover at least for six weeks, sometimes two months, in advance of that holiday. She worked terribly hard. Everything had to be turned inside out in the house-- the cleaning, the spring cleaning. Everything happened for Passover. So it was a very festive occasion for the children. But I remember that even then, I felt bad for my mother because of the hard work that was involved in it. But she took it stoically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents who shared in the holiday with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My grandparents on my father's side-- I don't remember my grandfather on my father's side. He had died before I was born. I do remember somewhat of my grandmother on my father's side. I remember going to her funeral. I must have been about 3 years old. And I still have an image of what she looked like before she died. That was on my father's side. On my mother's side, I remember my grandmother had died before I was born, also. But my grandfather on my mother's side lived in a town nearby, which was called Pionki. And we went to visit him quite often. And we used to spend the summers there at my grandfather's house. But I never remember him being to my house, because I think at that point, he was too old to travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your siblings all go to the same school as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At different times, we went to different schools. But there was this one school in Radom, which was called [NON-ENGLISH] And that was the principal educational tool, you might say, for the children in Radom. They had both. They had a high school, private high school, and a grade school attached to it. Now, my oldest brother went to the high school at the time when I recall. And-- --my sister never went to that school. She went to another school, to a girls school. My middle brother, Eli, he went to a different school-- also Jewish, private. And I went-- for the fifth and sixth grade. I went to this [NON-ENGLISH] the grade school, which I was then to follow into high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told not to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. This is a very good question. Very few Jewish boys or girls had non-Jewish friends, because this great divide between Jews and Poles, Gentile Poles, it was so great that it was impossible to make friends. And I don't particularly blame them or us. It was just that way. The divide was just too great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see-- did you ever witness, growing up, acts of violence between Gentiles and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, many. I myself was accosted many times by Catholic Gentile boys, who would either threaten me or sometimes beat me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can remember since I was 3 or 4 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the boys when you were 3 or 4 who hurt you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It really didn't matter. You know, Jewish boys couldn't fight back, and wouldn't fight back, because this was inculcated in you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to fight?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to fight back. And the reason not to fight back was probably what happened to the Jewish people for the last 500 years. It was this-- the fear of starting a pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate your bar  mitzvah in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, as a matter of fact, I did. I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the celebration like? And please tell us again your birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: The birthday was November 15, which made my bar mitzvah under the Germans, because the Germans entered the city of Radom on September 8. My bar mitzvah was not until November 15, or thereabouts. So yes, my parents insisted we make a bar mitzvah, some kind of a rite. I describe it in one of my stories. Yes, we had a bar mitzvah rite in an-- in an apartment that was owned by a rabbi who lived in the apartment complex that we lived in. And he had created a room that was camouflaged with a wardrobe so that no one would know that there was an entry-- there's a room there. And it was in this room that we went in to pray, because praying, of course, under the Germans was forbidden under the pain of death immediately, as soon as they entered. And-- but my parents insisted, and we do go through this ritual. And we went into this room in the rabbi's house that was camouflaged with this wardrobe. And when I was about to begin reciting the haftarah, Germans came in. And we heard on the outside the-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shuffling?

speaker: interviewee
text: --rough shuffling with the screams-- Juden raus, Juden raus. And everybody froze. And the Germans actually came into the room, to the apartment where the rabbi lived, and they asked loudly, 'where are the males? Where are the men?' Because they were-- they were-- at that point it was early in the war. They would come down and raid the Jewish neighborhoods and grab males so that they would work for them and abuse them for whatever reasons they had. So they were on one of these raids that they came into that apartment. Well, we were very close to having someone come in there, because they actually went into the-- opened the wardrobe doors from the other side, and one of them went into the wardrobe to look. But somehow, he didn't find. And so eventually, they left. And I asked the people that were present there if I should continue reading the haftarah. And everybody-- yes, said yes, go ahead and do it. And so this is how we celebrated my bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was there, other than your parents and the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few friends, some neighbors. Very close people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My siblings, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to tell us about your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The thing that stands out is that moment of fear-- terrible, terrible fear, where the German was rummaging in that wardrobe, and we thought that he was going to get wise to the fact that there was a door behind that. And somehow, it didn't happen. So this is the only thing I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me, please, about your siblings, how old they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My brother, Abram, was born in 1919. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] He was a very bright young man. He graduated either number two or number three at the head of his class in high school, in a school which attracted the best brains in the city. So for him to be number two or number three is really a great accomplishment. He was great in physics and math, and the whole family believed that he was going to be a famous scientist one day. We were really all convinced of that. His schooling came to an abrupt end in 1938, when he graduated high school. He was accepted to the Polish officers school, which was the routine rite of every high school graduate in those days. But they wouldn't accept him because-- they wouldn't take him because of the fact that he was Jewish. So in 1938, with the brains that he had, his career came to a sudden end. And the family decided that the only way that we can handle this would be to accumulate enough money and eventually send him out of the country to continue his studies. And we would have done that if the war had not interfered. My sister, Bluma, was a very pretty girl. She was the only girl in the household. And she enjoyed all kinds of privileges because of that. She was the apple of my father's eye. She was a very pretty girl. I have a picture of her. And she went to private school. But in that generation, the education of a girl was not all that important. So her school career ended with grade school, as far as I can remember. And I don't remember that we had any plans for her to continue high school, although we might have. I don't remember that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The most beautiful part about my sister was that she never left the side of my mother and father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] --she stayed with them throughout, even though she could have saved her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the second born, and she was born in 1921. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can say that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as far as being heroic, she was the one that was most heroic of us all. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --your next sibling?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Eli. He was born in 1923. And Eli was what we all know and call a tough guy. He couldn't take the abuse from the Gentile boys, and he didn't. He fought back. He was constantly in trouble. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In trouble with your father or in trouble with--

speaker: interviewee
text: In trouble with everybody. He was in trouble with my father because he had the wrong friends. And the reason he had the wrong friends was because he always wanted to fight back, because he couldn't take the abuse. If a Polish kid called him dirty Jew, he couldn't let him get away with it. And that created all kinds of problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was constantly in fights. And he'd come home with black and blue marks, with eyes, with torn pants and torn jackets, and all kinds of wounds. He was in constant trouble and he was constantly fighting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, he eventually paid for his toughness with his life under the Germans, because he resisted a foreman on the job, after the foreman had ridiculed him to the point where he couldn't take it anymore, and he hit back. Because he hit back, he was tortured and then shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was it. I had two brothers, Abram, Eli, and one sister, Bluma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, as a-- a a youngster, have any awareness of the rise of Nazism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your awareness? Did you hear your parents talking? I was very much aware, even though at the young and tender age, because I wanted to stay close to my father, and I wanted to have discussions with him. And I noticed that he discussed such things with my older siblings. And I wanted very badly to be able to take part in it. And so at a very young age, I started reading newspapers. And I made it my business to know. And I might say that when I look back at it that I did know a lot of what was happening. And I was aware of the situation. At around what age, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think at the age of 10, I was very much aware of what was going on. And I was able to hold a conversation with my father and hold my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: What newspapers would you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one particular newspaper. It called [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this a Polish newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish newspaper, but it was edited by Jews. It was actually a Jewish newspaper in Polish. And I was comfortable reading Polish. I wasn't comfortable reading Yiddish, although they were at least a half a dozen Yiddish newspapers in our city as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I picked up this [NON-ENGLISH] and I might say to you in what way. I remember in 1938, my father would come out to Pionki in the summertime for the weekend. And I would go to the train station to wait for him. And as I waited for him, I would pick up this newspaper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember distinctly that I read the paper very thoroughly so that I could discuss it with him, the contents of what I read, on our way home, as we walked home. And I knew that, did my father, gave him a lot of pleasure. And so yes, I remember. I was aware. And he would correct me every once in a-- I was aware.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that conversation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I have it written verbatim, some of it. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you share it with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only way I could is if I dig it out and-- is it all right? [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I have them under chapters. OK. This chapter is called Pionki, which is the name of the place that we're talking about. And-- [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] 'There was much-- much talk about'-- saying that I needed to talk to my father on the way home. 'Nazi Germany was rearming at a frantic pace. Chamberlain and Deladier were playing the appeasement game with Herr Hitler, and England issued a white paper denying Jews the right to go to Palestine. Polish hooligans were throwing stink bombs at the Jewish business establishments, and otherwise preventing people from shopping in Jewish stores. When these practices were challenged in the Polish courts, the ruling was the infamous [NON-ENGLISH], meaning that picketing is legal. Polish-born Jews were being booted out of Germany, their possessions confiscated, and sent to Zbaszyn, a Polish-German border town. 'America was in a deep economic depression then, politically isolationist, with Nazis marching in the streets under swastika banners. I discussed all those events with my father, but he was an optimist. 'Things always look darkest before dawn,' he said. 'Jewish people have had to endure Hamans throughout history, and somehow we survive. Hitler is just another Haman. He will exact a toll, but in the long run, we will persevere.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 'And I said, 'But Father, why is the whole world against us? Why is there so much hatred?'

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Can't we do something to defend ourselves? And why, I ask, can we not leave-- --this godforsaken place full of hatred and bigotry?' 'When barraged with such questions, he would pet my head, and his eyes would become sad. 'To leave now would require a lot of money, which we don't have. Besides, how can we leave our place of birth, where everyone knows us, our friends, our relatives? The little we have sustains us here. And it is more than we can hold in a new country in these times of world economic depression. No, my son,' he said. 'Our strength is in our faith and in our Bible.' '

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. Would you like a drink? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: ' 'Our strength is in our faith and in our Bible. We are the people of the book, even though sometimes that is not enough. That is why we're trying to establish a state in Palestine. If not in my lifetime, then perhaps in yours, that will become a reality.' ' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was the conversation. That was what we talked about, and that was the state, that I remember, of mind of my father's generation and my generation. My generation began to look towards Palestine as the redeeming-- the place of redemption, the place to go to. We knew that under no circumstances can we continue to live in this godforsaken place where everybody hated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's generation was somewhat different. They-- they were more practical, of course. They knew that the world is not an oyster, and nobody's exactly waiting for Jews, to welcome them with open arms. And so he knew from practical experience that the little that we have needs to be preserved and cherished.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to the best of your recollection, did your father ever make an attempt to leave Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And I've thought about that often. And I really can't blame him, because I'm now in a situation where I can identify with him. My father was 52 when he perished. And at 52, you think not in terms of a 15- or 16-year-old. You think in terms of someone who has the sole responsibilities to sustain a family. And on that basis, I cannot blame him. He had his connections. He had his past. He had his present. He has the future right there. He knew how to make the most of the terrible situation that we were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he also knew that he would always manage to sustain the family where he was. How can I blame him for not leaving this place and going into a world that was hostile to Jews anyway, and starting from scratch at that age? I can't blame him for that. Do you remember any family vacations growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of the family vacations that I remember were in Pionki, because this was the place that my grandfather had an estate-- not an estate, a few acres of land with a couple of buildings on it. And it was a nice place, and I have excellent memories from the summers that we spent there. Mostly-- the few summers before the war that I can recall were spent in Pionki-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --in my grandfather's house. Could you tell me about one of those summers, what you did, how you amused yourself? Were there other children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were other children there. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --the hatred of-- the divide between the Poles and the Jews was not as great, somehow, in Pionki, at least not immediately, because the people who lived in the buildings that belonged to my grandfather considered me their landlord, or whatever. So they had, somehow, a little respect. And I was not physically afraid of anybody on a daily basis, like I was back in Radom. Back in Radom, as soon as I left my house, I went downstairs, I had to watch out for these hooligans, for the Polish boys. In Pionki, I was a little more relaxed. And I had wonderful, wonderful days-- wonderful, most wonderful days. There was a big lake in Pionki where my father taught me how to swim. And I had friends, and I would leave the house about 8 in the morning every day and go to that lake and spend it with my friends, and come back at 2 o'clock in the afternoon to have a bite to eat, and then run right back to that lake. And we had a little boat that I and a friend of mine built. His father was in the hardware business. He had a hardware store, so he supplied us with all of the necessary tools and things that we needed. And we built a boat, and we actually launched it. And we had fun with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a sailboat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, it was a paddle boat. But it was good. It was great. It was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your whole family was there with you in Pionki? Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Intermittently. My mother was there always. My sister was there always. But my brothers would come and go. They were not there all the time. And of course I was the youngest, and I was there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
